nodes.text: You've seen it, then. The prescriber and patient name. I was going to mention that — I thought I already mentioned that.

responses.text: You did. I need to ask you about it formally.
The system has flagged this automatically.

nodes.text: Of course. Ask away. I've got nothing to hide — it's all documented, my employment records, my prescription history, everything. I've been a GP here for nineteen years. This is not typical for me.

responses.text: A physician prescribing a Schedule II controlled substance to themselves is prohibited under federal law.
Can you confirm your date of birth?

nodes.text: I see. Well, that's the software doing its job, I suppose. For what it's worth, the situation is temporary and documented. My partner is back from Vancouver next week. I just needed the coverage.

responses.text: Documentation doesn't change the regulatory issue.
Can you confirm your date of birth while I review this?

nodes.text: I'm aware of the regulation. I'm also a physician in good standing asking you to apply some common sense. I've been coming here — well, not here specifically, but to pharmacies like this — for twenty years. I know what responsible prescribing looks like.

responses.text: I understand. But I can't fill this.

nodes.text: March twenty-second, nineteen seventy-two. Fifty-one years old. I've been a doctor for half of those years, for whatever that's worth.

responses.text: That matches. I still need to address the prescriber issue.

nodes.text: I see. I'm going to ask you to reconsider. I'm asking as a colleague.
